title: Heapify, insert and extract

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Heapify, insert and extract; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Algorithmic Foundations; August 2023; material applicable to Heapify, insert and extract
sources.claim: Heap insertion bubbles a new element upward, removal replaces the root and restores order downward, and heap construction can run in linear time bottom-up.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Heap insertion bubbles a new element upward, removal replaces the root and restores order downward, and heap construction can run in linear time bottom-up.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: After removing the minimum, the last element moves to root and sifts down toward the appropriate child. Your goal is to implement and test heap construction, insertion and root extraction. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Array representation and operations; Tree terminology and representation; Heap property and array layout; Correctness and counterexamples. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: Heap insertion bubbles a new element upward, removal replaces the root and restores order downward, and heap construction can run in linear time bottom-up. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: In C, tree nodes and heap buffers need explicit lifetime and bounds; C++ can combine RAII nodes with vector heaps; Java uses object references and PriorityQueue contracts; Python uses objects plus heapq's min-heap functions, while the conceptual invariant stays unchanged.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Comparator consistency, index bounds, and mutable priorities can invalidate the structure. The draft therefore rejects: Building a heap by bottom-up sifting is O(n log n).

includes: Restore heap order upward or downward after structural changes.
Implement and test heap construction, insertion and root extraction.

In C, tree nodes and heap buffers need explicit lifetime and bounds; C++ can combine RAII nodes with vector heaps; Java uses object references and PriorityQueue contracts; Python uses objects plus heapq's min-heap functions, while the conceptual invariant stays unchanged.
excludes: Comparator consistency, index bounds, and mutable priorities can invalidate the structure.

outcomes: Implement and test heap construction, insertion and root extraction.

examples.title: Heapify, insert and extract: language-neutral trace
examples.situation: After removing the minimum, the last element moves to root and sifts down toward the appropriate child.
walkthrough: Step 1 — State the abstract input and invariant: After removing the minimum, the last element moves to root and sifts down toward the appropriate child.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Bottom-up heap construction is O(n); repeated insertion is the O(n log n) alternative.
